questions.q: Why is feature scaling important for distance-based algorithms like k-NN and SVM?
options: It reduces the training time by a factor of k
Without scaling, features with larger ranges dominate the distance calculation, making small-range features irrelevant
It guarantees that the model will achieve 100% accuracy
It is only necessary for classification, not regression
questions.explanation: If one feature ranges 0-1000 and another 0-1, the large-range feature dominates Euclidean distance. Standardization (mean 0, std 1) or normalization (0-1 range) equalizes feature contributions. Tree-based models are invariant to scaling; linear models, SVM, k-NN, and neural networks benefit significantly.

questions.q: What is one-hot encoding and when is it used?
options: A way to reduce the dimensionality of continuous features
Converting categorical variables into binary vectors where each category becomes a separate binary feature
A regularization technique that randomly zeros out features during training
A method to detect missing values in the dataset
questions.explanation: One-hot encoding transforms a categorical feature with k categories into k binary columns (or k-1 to avoid multicollinearity). For example, color={red, green, blue} becomes three binary features. This allows algorithms like logistic regression and SVM (which require numerical input) to handle categorical data.

questions.q: What is feature selection and why is it important?
options: Choosing between linear and non-linear models
Identifying and keeping only the most relevant features to improve model interpretability, reduce overfitting, and decrease computation
Scaling features to have the same range
Training multiple models on different subsets of features
questions.explanation: Feature selection removes irrelevant or redundant features. Methods include filter approaches (correlation, chi-squared), wrapper approaches (RFE, backward elimination), and embedded approaches (L1 regularization). Fewer features reduce overfitting, training time, and improve interpretability while often maintaining or improving performance.

questions.q: What does Principal Component Analysis (PCA) do?
options: Removes outliers from the dataset
Transforms high-dimensional data into lower dimensions by finding new axes (principal components) that maximize variance
Automatically selects the best-performing features for the model
Converts categorical variables to numerical features
questions.explanation: PCA finds orthogonal directions of maximum variance. The first principal component captures the most variance, the second (uncorrelated) captures the second most, and so on. Retaining the top k components reduces dimensionality while preserving most information. Variance explained by each component guides how many to keep.

questions.q: How should missing values typically be handled in machine learning?
options: Always delete rows with missing values
Delete rows with any missing value, or impute (fill) using mean, median, mode, forward-fill, or model-based methods depending on the context
Replace all missing values with zero
Missing values automatically disappear during model training
questions.explanation: Deleting rows loses data; imputation preserves them. Mean/median works for MCAR (missing completely at random) data. Forward-fill suits time-series. More sophisticated methods: KNN imputation, multiple imputation (MICE), or model-based approaches. Strategy depends on missing data mechanism and domain knowledge.

questions.q: What is the difference between standardization and normalization?
options: They are the same thing, just different names
Standardization (z-score) centers data (mean 0, std 1) using (x - μ) / σ; normalization (min-max) rescales to [0, 1] using (x - x_min) / (x_max - x_min)
Standardization is only for categorical data; normalization is for numerical
Normalization removes outliers; standardization does not
questions.explanation: Standardization is preferred when the scale is unknown or unbounded. Normalization is better when you know the feature bounds and want a specific range. Min-max normalization is sensitive to outliers (they can squeeze other values into a small range). Standardization is more robust.

questions.q: What are polynomial features and when might you use them?
options: Features created by multiplying the polynomial coefficients together
Creating new features as powers and interactions of existing features (e.g., x, x², x₃, x·y) to capture non-linear relationships
Removing features that are not polynomially distributed
A way to perform regression on categorical data
questions.explanation: Polynomial features enable linear models (e.g., linear regression) to fit non-linear relationships. For example, adding x² allows fitting a parabola. Interaction terms (e.g., x·y) capture feature dependencies. Caution: they increase dimensionality and can cause overfitting; regularization is often needed.

questions.q: What is dimensionality reduction and what problem does it solve?
options: Removing rows from the dataset to make it smaller
Reducing the number of features while preserving relevant information, combating the curse of dimensionality and improving computational efficiency
Replacing continuous features with categorical ones
It serves no practical purpose in machine learning
questions.explanation: High-dimensional data suffers from sparsity, computational expense, and overfitting. Dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE, autoencoders) compresses data to fewer dimensions. This improves visualization, speeds up training, and can reduce noise. Information loss is the trade-off; choosing the number of dimensions requires careful validation.

questions.q: What is feature interaction and how does it help?
options: Ensuring all features have the same scale
Creating new features as products or combinations of existing features to capture relationships that linear combinations alone cannot express
Removing correlated features from the dataset
Splitting a dataset into features and labels
questions.explanation: Feature interactions model non-additive relationships. For example, in a decision tree, the effect of feature A on the outcome depends on the value of feature B. Manually creating interaction terms (x₁·x₂) or letting tree-based models discover them automatically can significantly improve predictions.

questions.q: What is target encoding and when is it used instead of one-hot encoding?
options: Encoding the target variable as 0/1 for binary classification
Replacing categorical feature values with the mean target value for each category; useful when a categorical feature has many unique values
Removing features that are highly correlated with the target
A preprocessing step that is always better than one-hot encoding
questions.explanation: Target encoding replaces a categorical feature's categories with the mean target value observed for each category. This is compact (one numerical column vs. k binary columns) and often predictive, especially for high-cardinality features. Risk: target leakage if not cross-validated properly. One-hot is safer but creates more features.
